did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Neural Architecture Search (NAS) and Model Quantization with deep convolutional neural networks (specifically EfficientNet) to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset, achieving a weighted average F1-score of 0.93.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the proposal of a novel methodology using Neural Architecture Search and Model Quantization for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods (HAM10000 dataset, EfficientNet, Quantization Aware Training), reports original results (F1-score of 0.93, model size reduction), and compares them against existing models, which characterizes it as a research paper rather than a review.

title: Detection Of Skin Cancer Using Neural Architecture Search with Model Quantization
journal: Proceedings of the Fifth International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Control Systems (ICICCS 2021)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel methodology for skin cancer detection by combining Neural Architecture Search (NAS) with Model Quantization to create an efficient classifier deployable on low-compute devices. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset of dermatoscopic images and employed NAS to automatically identify an effective deep convolutional neural network architecture, specifically EfficientNet. To optimize the model for edge deployment, Quantization Aware Training was applied, which reduced the model size by approximately four times while maintaining accuracy. The model was evaluated using precision, recall, and F1-scores on the test dataset, achieving a weighted average F1-score of 0.93. Comparative analysis demonstrated that the proposed quantized model outperformed several existing state-of-the-art models in terms of overall and per-class accuracy. The results indicate that this approach effectively balances model size reduction with high diagnostic performance, making it suitable for mobile and web applications.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion diagnostic categories (Actinic keratoses, Basal cell carcinoma, Benign keratosis-like lesions, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic nevi, Vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper builds a skin cancer classifier using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic classes for skin lesions. Performance is evaluated using Precision, Recall, and F1-Scores for each of the seven lesion classes, indicating a multi-class classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: The 'Human Against Machine with 10000 training images' dataset, a large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions. The original images were resized from 600x450 to 300x300 and normalized by dividing pixel values by 255.0.

use.training: Used as the primary dataset for training the skin cancer classifier. The study utilized 8000 image samples per epoch for training with a batch size of 32, applying data augmentation techniques including resizing, normalization, random horizontal/vertical flipping, random height/width shifts, zoom, and random rotation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the final model's performance, reporting precision, recall, and F1-scores for each lesion type and generating a confusion matrix.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

HAM10000.experiment: Proposed Model (NAS with EfficientNet + Quantization Aware Training)
HAM10000.notes: The base model size was reduced from 41.59658432006836 Mb to 10.701797485351562 Mb (a 74.28% reduction) after quantization. The accuracy decreased by approximately 6.5% (from 93.1% to 86.5%). The model was evaluated on a test dataset of 1002 images. The HAM10000 dataset is noted to be highly imbalanced.

Comparison with Existing Models.notes: The proposed model outperformed the listed existing architectures in terms of average precision, recall, and F1 score.

hyperparameters_during_search.validation_data_subset: 100 randomly chosen images from test set

conclusions: The proposed methodology combining Neural Architecture Search (NAS) and Model Quantization successfully creates a skin cancer classifier deployable on low-compute devices.
The quantized model achieved a weighted average F1-score of 0.93, outperforming existing state-of-the-art models such as Densenet-121, MobileNet, VGG16, VGG19, and InceptionV3.
Model quantization reduced the model size by approximately 74.28% (from ~41.6 MB to ~10.7 MB), making it suitable for devices with limited memory and computational capacity.
The cost of quantization was a decrease in accuracy from 93.1% to 86.5% (a ~6.5% drop), which the authors consider a minimal reduction given the significant size reduction.
The model's performance varied across lesion classes, which the authors attribute to the high class imbalance inherent in the HAM10000 dataset.
The study demonstrates that NAS can effectively identify efficient architectures for specific medical imaging tasks, rather than relying solely on pre-existing general-purpose architectures.
Future work includes deploying and testing the classifier on web and mobile applications to further validate its utility in clinical settings.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is explicitly noted as highly imbalanced, potentially limiting generalizability to other populations or datasets.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 test set, raising concerns about clinical applicability without independent validation.
Model quantization resulted in a significant accuracy drop from 93.1% to 86.5%, which the authors characterize as 'minimal' but represents a substantial performance degradation.

The Neural Architecture Search (NAS) was conducted on a very small subset of data (1000 training images and 100 test images) with limited trials (100) and epochs (5), which may not robustly represent the full search space.

Comparisons with existing models (e.g., Densenet-121, MobileNet) appear to use aggregated average metrics from cited literature rather than re-evaluating those models on the same test split, limiting the validity of direct performance comparisons.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests or confidence intervals for the reported metrics.
Deployment is proposed for future work; no actual clinical or edge-device deployment testing is reported in this study.
The model's performance on Melanoma (F1: 0.74) is notably lower than on benign classes (e.g., Melanocytic nevi F1: 0.97), highlighting potential risks in false negatives for the most critical class.
